author: @ryanjamesloyd6733
text:
So this has been kicking around in my head the last couple days, and a thought keeps nagging at me concerning onboard generation- 
Would a 'generation at the rim" speed rather than torque based generator, if hooked to a small internal combustion engine, perhaps get you more bang for your fuel buck?  I know getting away from fossil fuels is the point but using Less is a step in the right direction.    I just wonder how much, say, a string trimmer or chainsaw motor (or something less dirty, or run it on propane, or wood gas, or whatever) could generate with that generator concept?  Enough, perhaps, to drive electric hub motors?    
I'm just thinking if that concept makes small wind turbines functionally productive in light winds, then it could Also make fuel driven generation more efficient in terms of output per fuel- requiring either a Smaller engine, or just less consumption.  I don't know of course, it just Seems like that would correlate.  
(And as stirling and steam can be more efficient than ICE-  That conversion from fuel to electricity to actually run the vehicle, to my mind, might make sense. )

I dunno, it's just been keeping me up at night so I thought I'd mention it.

author: @craigglewis
text:
The concept of micro-generation, where small-scale energy sources are used to generate power locally, is indeed an interesting and evolving area. It involves harnessing energy from various sources, such as renewable energy systems or innovative technologies like micro generators, to produce electricity on a smaller scale.

Your idea of using micro-generation to feed a larger hydrogen generator, which in turn powers vehicles or provides energy for homes, aligns with the broader goal of decentralized and sustainable energy solutions. This approach could potentially offer more flexibility and efficiency in energy use.

As technology continues to advance, and the demand for clean and decentralized energy grows, exploring innovative solutions like the one you mentioned becomes increasingly relevant. If you have specific questions or aspects you'd like to delve into further, feel free to share, and we can discuss it in more detail!

author: @craigglewis
text:
Yes, sometimes it's easier to explain things to people then answer everyone's questions lol
 I'm glad to hear what you're planning and micro generation and hydrogen is going to be the future. I think and the possibilities are here we can do that now for sure.  

Things are going to get exciting Robert,😁

Stanley, Tesla and Rob.

Tesla Coil Hubless Wheels
Stanley Hydrogen Generator Robert Graphene Plates
Rob, long may you live, Sir.

Quad Bike and Camper Unit.
"The Electric Nomad"

author: @VeniceInventors
text:
I'm not sure onboard electricity generation is worth the tradeoffs, as it adds weight and complexity, at least in the case of a fuel or hydrogen tank and generator or fuel cell. Then there are conversion losses, and the added overhead and inefficiencies stemming from the production, storage and delivery of the fuel.
By comparison, using batteries only, seems much simpler and straight to the point. 
What would really make all the difference is some actual breakthrough in battery technology. By that I mean a complete departure from the old anode/electrolyte/cathode approach that has been in use for almost 200 years and was possibly first conceived thousands of years ago. Chemists, physicists, engineers and inventors need to think outside of the box for that one.

author: @lorenedailey8691
text: Connect an exercise trampoline with (video 2005, child's play generator), a (universal planetary gearing, video 1964), and a (more powerful and more efficient generator, video 1931). (links) https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BSsj2rDJ2ts&t=95s    https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JpXS53_iqj8  https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BX7UgVSUOBE&t=10s  Put a return spring on the child's play generator, make the rotor and stator larger in the video i931 generator, increase the speed on your gearing, and now you have your power system. You can make it mobile and go bouncing along in your e-qiuad.

author: @orpheuscreativeco9236
text:
I was rewatching your super capacitor videos and had this realization : a generator/regenerative braking system (based on some type of clutching gears in my mind) combined with a super capacitor bank would provide just enough juice for pedal assistance. In my mind you could put the bike on a motorcycle type kickstand that lifts the back wheel off the ground, and toggle the linkage to allow for the regenerative braking system to be partially engaged so that one could precharge for a trip or just exercise and charge a USB device. I don't know if this whole thing would get too heavy for it to be beneficial, but it's just in the dream phase now. 😁👍

Thanks for sharing Rob! ✌️

author: @mikejones-vd3fg
text: was just watching a video of a guy from brasil who made a steam powered trike... all you need is wood and water and youre on the move!   Never thought to DIY my own vehicle and dont have the shop to do it anyways so converting bikes or just making battery packs is as far as im willing to go at the moment, but man i have a whole lot of videos on this subject ive been watching.
